By Shawn Twing
Everything you’ve learned so far in the �rst �ve paid traf�c
lessons has set the stage for lesson six.
This is where we add the jet fuel to our SOI-powered traf�c
engine.
In lesson �ve I mentioned problems with binary, yes/no choices.
The biggest problem is that when confronted with a yes/no
choice, most people will say no.
We can certainly ask the same or similar question again, or make
the same or similar offer again (and again and again…), but at
some point we have to step back and ask if there’s a better way.
There is — and it’s behaviorally-based retargeting. If you’re already
familiar with retargeting DO NOT skip this section. We’re going
to use retargeting very differently than most advertisers do and
that means you’re going to set yourself up for much better results.
The Secret Sauce: Behaviorally-
Based Retargeting
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 18 --

The Typical Approach
Here’s the typical way retargeting is used. Visit website A and
leave. Open Facebook and whatever you were just barely looking
at on website A is there in your feed:
▪ “Did you forget something?”
▪ “How can we help?”
▪ “Hey, this thing you just looked at ten seconds ago is still available…”
So annoying.
Let’s take a moment and describe what’s really happening.
Something piqued my curiosity enough to visit a website. When I
got there, I looked around and decided I wasn’t interested enough
to buy (or to opt-in), and I left.
Does it make sense to ask me if I’m sure about that decision ten
seconds after I made it?
Because what the advertiser really is saying is:
“Hey, I noticed you were on my website ten seconds ago and didn’t
buy, and I’d really like you to come back and buy right now …
seriously, right now … how about now?”
I know the arguments — people get distracted, recency is
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 18 --

important, etc. But let’s be honest and admit this is not a sensible
way to get the results we’re looking for.
There’s a much better way, and that’s what we’re going to discuss
here. Not only is it a better way, in general, but it’s a better way
that leverages the unique capabilities that an SOI-inspired
multi-page presell site provides.
The Better Approach
Let me explain.
In lesson �ve I mentioned the importance of the page one to
page two metric (your meaningful signal).
Page-one is the gatekeeper, and it’s our 6′ 8″, 360lb former Division
1 linebacker bodyguard for our MPPS.
If someone clicks on an ad, see page-one, and doesn’t make it
to page two we’re not going to retarget them at all.
WHAT?
Not retarget a site visitor at all?
Correct.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 18 --

Rule #1 in this lesson is this — people vote with their behavior.
When someone clicks and ad and doesn’t proceed past page one of
your MPPS, they have cast their vote and it said “not interested.”
We’re going to respect that, for their bene�t and for ours.
How?
No retargeting ads on Facebook or the Google Display Network
for page-one-only visitors, and we’re going to put all of that
wasteful ad spend right back into our budget for interacting with
the visitors who have told us they’re more interested.
Let this idea really sink in for a moment.
We’re only going to continue to interact with those people who
have given us a clear signal they’re more likely to become
quali�ed leads (and/or customers), and we’re going to ignore
everyone else.
We can make this simple or complicated.
I’ll explain the complicated version �rst and later discuss where
and how we can simplify.
There are guidelines — not rules.
I want you to understand how to think about this, then develop a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 18 --

strategy that’s appropriate to your MPPS, your offer, your
business, your audience, and your available resources.
Let’s imagine you have a �ve-page MPPS and you’re driving
traf�c from Google search and Facebook.
Let’s also assume that each page in your MPPS represents a belief
your prospect needs to accept before s/he can accept your overall
value proposition. The ad and page progression would look like
this:
Google or Facebook Ads
▪ Belief #1 (page 1)
▪ Belief #2 (page 2)
▪ Belief #3 (page 3)
▪ Belief #4 (page 4)
▪ Conclusion + opt-in form (page 5).
We’ve already made the decision that visitors who see page one
and nothing else don’t get retargeted so we don’t need to worry
about page one.
However, if someone clicks through to page two, that’s a positive
BEHAVIORAL signal. They’ve voted with their behavior and
made a micro-commitment deeper into our world.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 18 --

We also recognize life gets in the way, distractions happen, etc.,
and not everyone is going to consume our MPPS start to �nish.
(Some will — and we LOVE those people.)
If someone sees page two, but doesn’t proceed to page three, we
begin retargeting them with ads focused on the belief established
on page three.
If someone makes it to page three but doesn’t proceed to page
four, we repeat the process except this time we’re moving them
toward page four.
If someone makes it to page four but not page �ve … you get the
idea.
And �nally, if someone makes it all the way from page one
through page �ve but doesn’t opt-in, our retargeting ads focus on
the bene�ts we have associated with whatever the opt-in offer is.
One quick clarifying comment — time isn’t a factor in this
process.
A prospect could see pages one and two on their �rst visit, three
on their third visit, then four, �ve, and opt-in on the next visit.
The whole point is that we’re systematically engaging with an
audience that has raised its hand to show interest and ignoring
everyone else.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 18 --

We also leave open the possibility that that page-one-only crowd
will reengage. They’ll continue to see our initial ads over time and
they can always come back to our MPPS.
In my experience, that does happen occasionally.
Someone sees an ad, reads the initial content but doesn’t proceed
to page two, isn’t retargeted, sees the initial ad again, skims page
two and proceeds to page two and beyond.
We’re not preventing that from happening — but we’re not
speci�cally trying to make that happen with our retargeting.
What if you have an eight-page MPPS? Or ten pages? Same
process with more layers of complexity (or you can simplify
which I’ll explain in a moment).
The speci�cs don’t matter as much as understanding the process.
The idea is to �rst gain attention, then look for the signal that
indicates a high-quality prospect, and then nurture that smaller
audience all the way through our MPPS.
One key point to understand is the relationship between the
beliefs established in your MPPS and the eventual offer you’re
making.
Using the example above, we have four beliefs and then the offer
is an opt-in.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 18 --

If I am convinced a prospect really needs to accept all four of
those beliefs, I need to hold their hand through all of the content
step by step before making the offer for the opt-in.
On the other hand, if I am making an exhaustive case for the opt-
in, and I’m reinforcing that with an email sequence later, I might
be less concerned about the prospect consuming all of my MPPS
content and be more willing to fast-track someone to the opt-in.
Your results will depend entirely on your approach / needs.
The Better Approach — Simpli�ed
Target One Network At a Time
Now the we know the complicated way, let’s talk about ways we
can simplify our retargeting strategy, then move in to some
examples to get you thinking about how to use this for your
MPPS.
The �rst way we can simplify requires that I back up a little and
explain something I should have mentioned earlier.
I’m assuming that you’re going to use retargeting on the Google
Display Network and on Facebook to maximize your impact.
That’s the most complicated (and impactful) way to implement
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 18 --

this retargeting strategy.
Having said that, the �rst way to simplify is to focus only on one
of the retargeting options (I would recommend starting with
Facebook).
It’s easier to develop the creative, messaging, etc. for Facebook OR
Google vs. both.
Cut Down on the Speci�city (at least
at �rst)
A second way to simplify is to minimize the number of
retargeting steps after page two.
Using our �ve-page MPPS above as an example, the simplest
retargeting strategy would be to send visitors who make it to page
two but don’t turn into leads to the opt-in page by emphasizing
the bene�ts of the opt-in. That’s a very basic implementation.
We maintain the page one to page two signal for higher quality
traf�c, and we treat that like a gatekeeper — anyone who makes it
past the gatekeeper is directed to the opt-in page.
I’ve seen this work very well if there are clear bene�ts associated
with the opt-in.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 18 --

For example, our �ve-page MPPS may setup an opt-in for a free
multi-part mini-course offer. The MPPS content establishes the
beliefs that are reinforced by the mini-course content (which then
leads to the full-blown offer).
If a prospect has shown enough interest and made the micro-
commitment to click through from page one to page two, that
might be enough to get the opt-in with the next interaction. It’s
impossible to know for sure without testing.
It’s also important to look at the data over time to make these
decisions.
If the bene�ts are clear enough to get the opt-in, and the email
sequence is powerful enough to set the stage for the eventual
offer, then it may make more sense �nancially to get the positive
signal from the page one to page two conversion and then focus
the remainder of your budget and effort on getting the opt-in.
On the other hand, there is a power in the process of building up
beliefs in the MPPS that I want to be cautious not to undermine.
This is a delicate balance and there is no one-size-�ts-all answer.
The best answer I can give you from my experience is to
understand the overall strategy, adjust it to your particular
project, and then test, test, test.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 18 --

A Few Other Ways to Simplify
There are other ways to use this strategy too if you’re interested in
being inventive. One I’ve used is the “table of contents approach”.
Here’s how it works.
After you’ve created your MPPS, create a separate, stand-alone
overview page that has the title of each page in your MPPS and a
one-paragraph description of the content.
At the end of each paragraph include a link to the full content
(which is a link to that page in your MPPS).
For example, an excerpt of a TOC page for SOI might look like
this:
▪ The Ugly Truth About Product Launches (Part 1 of 2)
“The PLF bubble has a puncture, and it’s hemorrhaging air. Change
is afoot. And it’s coming whether you agree with me or not.” [Link to
that page]
▪ Part 2: The Ugly Truth about Product Launches
“It’s impacting a number of once-heavy-hitters in the space —
industry giants who, coincidentally, are watching their businesses
tank while continuing to teach business and branding to the
unsuspecting masses.” [Link to that page]
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 18 --

Etc.
Once the TOC page is setup, anyone who sees page two and
doesn’t opt-in will be retargeted back to the TOC.
Some people (like me) want to see an overview to skim and then
choose our own adventure. Others want to jump around, or have a
way to consume all the content over time.
NOTE: This table of contents page is not visible on the front end
of your MPPS. It’s only visible through retargeting as a way to
consolidate a retargeting campaign e�ciently.
[OK, I’ll be honest — I do have an ulterior motive which is using the
TOC campaign for a Google Display Network campaign later too, but
that’s beyond the scope of this training.
Again, I want to reiterate it’s not the speci�c implementations I’m
discussing that really matter, it’s the deeper thinking behind them.
There’s no reason you couldn’t create a TOC-campaign for retargeting
and then try that as a front-end tra�c generation campaign for
another audience. Think of this as a Swiss Army Knife / Leatherman
multi-tool campaign you can use in many di�erent ways.]
Let’s take a step back and ask the obvious question — why would
we bother doing so much extra work when we’re already driving
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 18 --

traf�c with Facebook and/or Google campaigns.
This is a very important question. Everything has an opportunity
cost, and if we’re spending time pushing and pulling traf�c
through our MPPS we have to be certain that’s not time (and
money) better spent increasing the volume of traf�c coming into
our MPPS in the �rst place!
The �rst thing we need to understand is that high-quality traf�c
is a �nite resource.
There are a couple of important distinctions we’ll discuss in
lesson eight, but for the most part our POP is most likely small
and growing slowly.
(This isn’t true for every market but it is true for most.)
If we’re not serving a mass-market audience with an easily and
quickly consumable product, then we need to take great care to
get the most out of our potential audience.
The second thing to understand is that the nature of Facebook
traf�c requires a different approach to paid advertising.
The economics of Facebook just don’t work if we need an
immediate signi�cant result to �nancially (or otherwise) justify
our efforts.
By its very nature Facebook traf�c is passive, with only potential
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 18 --

interest. It’s a vast audience in many ways, but not necessarily
when we start slicing it up into segments small enough to target
effectively yet large enough to �nancially sustain a business.
Effective, behaviorally-based retargeting recognizes this
reality and engages with the Facebook audience in a way that
better matches their behavior.
Someone might be consuming content on a mobile device on the
way to the of�ce, during work hours, and then standing in line at
the grocery store. We’re accepting that reality, and creating our
process to maximize our opportunities to acquire and sustain
attention.
A third reason is more dif�cult to quantify but it’s something I’ve
observed frequently.
Engaging with an audience the way SOI teaches, and then
mirroring that engagement thoughtfully using a behaviorally-
based retargeting strategy establishes trust and builds rapport.
It’s a very different experience for a prospect to feel like their
interaction with your business isn’t, “Go here to buy stu�,” followed
by, “I noticed you didn’t buy stu� so why don’t you come back and buy
stu�,” over and over again.
Taking the time to create a process that is respectful of an
audience’s needs is a very powerful competitive advantage.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 18 --

There is a humility inherent in creating good content, showing
prospects that content, and then making it easier for them to
consume that content their way.
Again, this is dif�cult / impossible to quantify, but it is something
I have observed repeatedly working with clients.
Comments and other feedback often re�ect the idea that the
client has done something different, more respectful, more
thoughtful / engaging and that is a powerful type of positioning
to establish early in a prospect’s mind.
Putting Your Retargeting Plan Into
Action
Before we move on, let’s talk about an action plan for setting up
retargeting.
STEP 1
De�ne the objectives that trigger retargeting. This can be done in
Facebook and Google AdWords — AdWords uses audiences
created in Google Analytics for this purpose.
Retarget anyone who sees page two or deeper in your MPPS, but
do not retarget anyone who only sees page one. Trust me — it’s
OK.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 18 --

STEP 2
Create a minimally effective retargeting strategy for anyone who
sees page two or deeper in your MPPS. That will be slightly
different for everyone.
If your opt-in offer is strong, I would start with retargeting to the
opt-in page. If you really need prospects to consume all of your
content before the opt-in, then use a table of contents page. Even
better — do both.
STEP 3
Pick an advertising platform and execute your minimally effective
retargeting strategy. I recommend starting with Facebook
retargeting (even if all of your initial traf�c is coming from
AdWords).
STEP 4
Expand your retargeting strategy.
If you’re retargeting to opt-in only, add a TOC.
If you’re retargeting to opt-in and a TOC, �nd the page transition
in your funnel with the worst next page conversion rate and
retarget visitors from that page to the opt-in.
(I’ll talk more about MPPS reporting for paid tra�c in lesson seven — for
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 18 --

now, keep in mind that you’re probably going to lose visitors from page to
page the deeper they go into your MPPS and focusing on improving the
worst performer will produce the most impactful results.)
STEP 5
Expand retargeting into the Google Display Network if you’re
only using Facebook (or add Facebook if you’re only using the
GDN). Add the simplest elements of your retargeting strategy
�rst, and expand as time allows.
Is this a lot of work?
Yes and no. Once you wrap your mind around the speci�c ways to
use this for your business the rest is setup, production, and
measurement.
The highest-value time will be spent initially on the strategy that
�ts your MPPS best.
If that’s page-by-page-by-page, one to the next from page two
onward, then build that out step by step. Don’t wait until the
whole thing is �nished and then do it, build the components in
order and execute so you’re building on success every step of the
way.
Everything you do will generate data which can be mined for
insights, and that’s incredibly valuable even if the underlying
strategy is underperforming.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 18 --

The moment we’ve done something that can be reported against
is the moment we’re on our way to making improvements.
That’s where we’re going next.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 18 --
